planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in very well-drained, sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in zone 10–12; Red Latan Palm is a striking, slow-growing, large fan palm endemic to the island of Réunion in the Mascarene Islands — critically endangered in the wild — bearing enormous, circular, blue-green fan fronds with distinctive red midribs, petioles, and leaf sheaths in juvenile plants that give it an extraordinary color display unique among fan palms.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, sandy, lean to average, alkaline-tolerant soil; Latania lontaroides is adapted to well-drained coastal soils and tolerates salt spray, drought, and exposed coastal conditions — avoid waterlogged clay soils. Latania lontaroides (Red Latan Palm or Latanier Rouge) is a large, majestic, slow-growing fan palm critically endangered in its native habitat — it is endemic to the island of Réunion in the Indian Ocean Mascarene Islands, where remaining wild populations are severely threatened by habitat loss and invasive species. The palm is celebrated for one of the most spectacular color displays in the palm family: juvenile and young plants (up to 10–15 years old) bear enormous, circular fan fronds with vivid blood-red to crimson midribs, petioles, and sheaths that contrast dramatically against the blue-green frond blades — the color gradually fading to reddish-brown and then gray as plants mature. Even mature plants retain reddish-tinged frond bases and produce an imposing crown of large, slightly glaucous, waxy fan fronds on a stout, prominently ring-marked trunk. Latania lontaroides is dioecious; both male and female plants display the color. Widely cultivated in botanical gardens and by palm collectors worldwide for its extraordinary juvenile coloration. Well adapted to coastal limestone soils, salt spray, and drought conditions in zone 10–12.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, strongly acidic soil in deep shade in tropical climates (zone 11–12); Joey's Palm is a spectacular, nearly stemless, understory fan palm native to the lowland tropical rainforests of Peninsular Malaysia, Borneo, and Sumatra — one of the most extraordinary of all palms — producing enormous, undivided, diamond-shaped leaves up to 9 feet tall and 4 feet wide from what appears to be ground level, without a visible stem.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), consistently moist, well-drained soil in deep shade; Johannesteijsmannia altifrons requires high humidity (above 80%), constant warmth (above 65°F), and deep, forest-floor shade — it does not tolerate direct sun, cold, or any degree of drought; the large leaves shred in wind. Johannesteijsmannia altifrons (Joey's Palm, Daun Payung, or Umbrella Leaf Palm) is among the most extraordinary and visually striking plants in the entire palm family — a nearly acaulescent (essentially stemless) rain forest floor palm native to the lowland dipterocarp rainforests of Peninsular Malaysia, southern Thailand, Borneo, and Sumatra, whose defining characteristic is the production of enormous, undivided, diamond or leaf-shaped fronds — each up to 9 feet tall and 4 feet wide — that emerge directly from a short, near-underground stem, creating the appearance of enormous individual green 'sails' or parasols growing from the forest floor with no visible trunk. These massive, pleated, entire (undivided) fronds rival Victoria amazonica water lily pads as the largest leaves of any monocot. In the rainforest understory, the leaves form natural rain-collecting shelters used by various small animals. Johannesteijsmannia altifrons requires the most exacting tropical rainforest conditions of any commonly cultivated palm: deep shade, constant warmth, high humidity, consistent moisture, and strongly acidic soil — making it a challenging but spectacularly rewarding specimen for advanced tropical plant collectors in zone 11–12.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial to deep shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in zone 10–12; White Elephant Palm is an extremely rare, slow-growing, understory fan palm endemic to just two forest areas in peninsular Thailand (Khao Nor Chuchi in Krabi Province) — critically endangered in the wild — producing a crown of large, circular fan fronds with brilliant white undersides that flash silvery-white when the fronds move in the wind.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly to strongly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; Kerriodoxa elegans requires consistently moist, humid, shaded conditions — protect from direct sun, cold below 45°F, and dry winds; the white frond undersides are the primary ornamental feature and must be positioned where they can be viewed from beneath. Kerriodoxa elegans (White Elephant Palm or Elegant Kerriodoxa) is an exceptionally rare, slow-growing, monotypic fan palm endemic to an extremely restricted habitat: the lowland rainforests of Khao Nor Chuchi and one or two adjacent forest areas in Krabi Province, peninsular Thailand, making it one of the rarest palms in the world by natural distribution — classified as Critically Endangered by IUCN. The name 'White Elephant Palm' references both the rarity (white elephants being considered sacred and extremely rare in Thai culture) and the striking ornamental feature: large, circular fan fronds (2–3 feet across) with deep-green upper surfaces and brilliant, chalky-white to silver-white lower surfaces — when the fronds move in a breeze, the alternating flash of green and white is spectacular. The palm is short to medium-trunked, reaching 10–15 feet in cultivation over many years, with a compact, elegant habit. Primarily of interest to specialized tropical palm collectors and botanical gardens. Always purchase nursery-propagated specimens — never wild-collected.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings 12–18 inches long in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost in zone 8–12; Chaya (Tree Spinach) is one of the most productive and nutritious leafy green vegetables available for warm-climate gardens — a Mesoamerican semi-succulent shrub producing abundant, bright-green, large, nutritious leaves year-round in tropical and subtropical climates — but MUST be cooked for 20 minutes before eating as raw leaves contain toxic hydrocyanic glycosides.

directions: IMPORTANT: Cook leaves for 20 minutes before eating — raw chaya contains toxic hydrocyanic acid glycosides; also do not cook in aluminum cookware as the reaction can cause illness. Plant cuttings in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained, fertile soil; Cnidoscolus chayamansa grows vigorously in warm climates, producing abundant leaves for harvest within 90 days of planting. Cnidoscolus chayamansa (Chaya, Tree Spinach, or Mayan Spinach) is a vigorous, semi-succulent, fast-growing shrub in the Euphorbiaceae family native to the Yucatan Peninsula of Mexico and cultivated throughout Mesoamerica, the Caribbean, and increasingly in warm-climate gardens worldwide as one of the most productive and nutritious perennial leafy greens available for subtropical and tropical climates. The Maya civilization used chaya as a primary vegetable crop for thousands of years — and modern nutritional analysis confirms it is extraordinarily nutrient-dense: the leaves contain 2–3 times more protein, iron, calcium, and vitamin C than spinach (Spinacia oleracea), and are among the highest vitamin-K-containing vegetables known. Chaya is propagated exclusively from stem cuttings (it rarely sets seed in cultivation) and grows into a large, productive bush that can be harvested continuously throughout the year in warm climates — making it potentially the most productive perennial leafy green per square foot available for Gulf Coast, Florida, and subtropical gardens. CRITICAL WARNING: raw chaya leaves contain hydrocyanic acid glycosides that cause serious illness if eaten raw or undercooked — leaves must be boiled (not steamed or microwaved) for a minimum of 20 minutes and the cooking water discarded before eating.
sources: University of Florida IFAS — Cnidoscolus chayamansa (Chaya)
Maya Traditional Foods Research — Cnidoscolus chayamansa

planting.note: Divide clumps or plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial to deep shade in zone 9–12; Queen of Hearts Homalomena is a compact, shade-tolerant aroid from Southeast Asian forest floors bearing heart-shaped, glossy, deep-green leaves with distinctive red undersides and stems — extraordinarily low-maintenance and excellent for shaded indoor or outdoor positions in warm, humid climates.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; Homalomena rubescens tolerates deep shade remarkably well — it actually prefers the low-light conditions of a forest floor and will scorch in direct sunlight; water moderately and avoid overwatering by ensuring the growing medium is well-draining. Homalomena rubescens (Queen of Hearts, Homalomena, or Selva Homalomena) is a compact, clumping, rhizomatous aroid in the Araceae family native to the humid forest floors of Borneo, Sumatra, Peninsular Malaysia, and the Philippines, cultivated as a shade-tolerant ornamental houseplant and outdoor shade-garden plant in warm subtropical and tropical climates. The plant forms dense, tidy clumps of long-stalked, broadly heart-shaped to ovate, glossy, dark-green leaves — often with striking red or burgundy undersides and deep-red petioles that give the plant attractive two-tone coloration. Homalomena rubescens is among the most shade-tolerant of all commonly cultivated aroids — adapted to the near-darkness of deep rainforest floor conditions — and is one of the most effective and low-maintenance shade groundcovers and container plants for zone 9–12 outdoor gardens and indoor positions throughout the world. The genus has an unusual characteristic: many Homalomena species emit a strong, anise-like or spicy aromatic scent from their leaves when handled — a chemotaxonomic trait of the genus. An excellent and underutilized shade garden plant for subtropical Florida, Gulf Coast, and tropical landscapes.
sources: University of Florida IFAS — Homalomena cultivation
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Homalomena rubescens
Marie Selby Botanical Gardens — Araceae Collection

planting.note: Divide clumps or plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in deep shade in zone 10–12; Philippine Evergreen is a robust, clumping aroid native to the Philippines and Sulawesi — the wild parent species of the wide array of ornamental Aglaonema cultivars grown as houseplants worldwide — bearing attractive, lance-shaped, dark-green leaves with silver-gray variegation in a slow-growing, durable, extraordinarily shade-tolerant clump.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in deep shade to bright indirect light; Aglaonema commutatum is among the most shade-tolerant and low-light-tolerant plants in cultivation — thriving in conditions with as little as 25–50 foot-candles of light; avoid direct sun, cold drafts, and overwatering. Aglaonema commutatum (Philippine Evergreen or Chinese Evergreen) is a slow-growing, clumping, rhizomatous aroid native to the humid tropical forests of the Philippines and Sulawesi, and the wild ancestor of dozens of cultivated Aglaonema varieties grown as among the most popular and durable foliage houseplants and low-light landscape plants in the world. The wild species produces lance-shaped, dark-green leaves with attractive silver-gray markings and variegation patterns in variable degrees, typically forming clumps 1–3 feet tall with arching stems. Aglaonema commutatum and its cultivated hybrids are celebrated as among the most tolerant of all ornamental plants to low light, neglect, dry indoor air, and irregular watering — making them a first-choice recommendation for deep-shade landscape positions, dim interior corners, offices, shopping centers, and any low-light situation where most plants fail. The wild species has been intensively hybridized to create hundreds of ornamental cultivars ranging from silver-variegated to red-pink, orange, and multi-colored forms. Outdoors in zone 10–12, it performs as an excellent, very low-maintenance, deep-shade groundcover or accent plant. All Aglaonema parts contain calcium oxalate crystals — mildly toxic if ingested.
sources: University of Florida IFAS — Aglaonema cultivation
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Aglaonema commutatum
